meta.description: A typical board game night with 6 friends. Mixed chaos tolerance, one phone addict, one competitor who wants to focus, and a host keeping things together.

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Wait, who did Pat catch? I wasn't even watching. Let me see what actually happened before I vote.
marcus.internal_thought: It's early enough that I don't care yet, but if Pat's being sloppy with claims, I need to shut that down now before it becomes a pattern that derails the actual game.

jade.dialogue: Oh dude, YES — I totally saw that! Pat's got this one, vote it LEGIT everyone!
jade.internal_thought: This is exactly what I'm here for — the chaos is STARTING and I'm already hyped.

pat.dialogue: Okay, okay—I saw River literally mid-scroll like thirty seconds ago, don't even try to deny it! Who's voting legit?
pat.internal_thought: Good—caught River red-handed early, this sets the right tone and gets everyone paying attention to each other; first claim should land clean and get people excited.

river.internal_thought: Pat's right, someone definitely had their phone out. Easy vote.

timeline.title: Story Time
timeline.description: Get Tyler to tell a story about their childhood.

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Tyler, just tell a story or don't—but can we focus on the actual game? We're literally five minutes in and these things keep breaking my concentration.
marcus.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of manufactured chaos that derails strategy thinking; I don't care about watching Tyler perform for points when I should be analyzing the board state.

pat.dialogue: Oh, Tyler—perfect timing! Dude, you gotta hit us with a good one. Childhood trauma or hilarious disaster, I'm not picky!
pat.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of thing that gets people relaxed and talking early—Tyler's solid, he'll bring energy, and it sets a vibe where everyone feels like they can contribute without stakes being crazy high yet.

river.dialogue: Tyler's got stories for days. Go ahead.
river.internal_thought: Twenty points if he bites—but I'm not the one pushing. I'll listen though, see what he says.

diana.dialogue: So wait — who's claiming this? And how do we verify it's actually *about* childhood and not just... any story Tyler tells?
diana.internal_thought: This flash is vague as hell and probably designed to let someone farm easy points by goading Tyler into literally any narrative — I need to know the acceptance criteria before this gets messy.

votingMechanic.name: THE DICTATOR
votingMechanic.description: One player has been chosen. Their word is law.

marcus.dialogue: Wait, hold on—Pat told that story like two minutes ago? Or... actually, I wasn't paying full attention. Who's the dictator on this one?
marcus.internal_thought: This is exactly what I'm talking about—we're still in setup and standing missions are already piling up, pulling me out of thinking about actual strategy.

jade.dialogue: Wait wait wait — Pat, did you actually catch someone repeating?? That's such a random one to land! Who was it?
jade.internal_thought: Pat's killing it tonight and I'm here for watching them dominate, this is exactly the chaos I signed up for.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Pat already told that story? Um... I guess, yeah? I wasn't really paying attention but like, sounds familiar.
tyler.internal_thought: Did Pat actually repeat something or is he just grabbing points? Honestly I have no idea, I was checking my fantasy roster like 30 seconds ago.

pat.dialogue: Whoa, whoa—hold up. I literally just told that story like five minutes ago? Did nobody hear me, or are you guys just trying to mess with me right now?
pat.internal_thought: This is awkward because I genuinely don't remember repeating myself, and now everyone's watching to see if I'm gonna fight this or take the L—but also it's only been six minutes, why are we already calling me out on this? Marcus is checked out, Diana looks confused, and now the vibe just got weird.

river.dialogue: Yeah, that checks out. Pat's been quiet most of the night—probably remembering it from earlier.
river.internal_thought: Pat's actually right and didn't make a big deal about it, which is respect. The Dictator rule means I don't have to argue anyway, so whatever.

diana.dialogue: Hold on — Pat, did you actually tell that story before tonight, or are you just claiming it because you think you did? And who's the Dictator here? Because if it's someone who wasn't paying attention, this whole thing falls apart.
diana.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of vague bullshit that ruins games — standing missions require evidence, not just confidence, and 'The Dictator' is a recipe for arbitrary rulings.

votingMechanic.name: Volunteer Tribunal
votingMechanic.description: Who wants to judge? First volunteers become the jury.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Pat, what—someone apologized? I wasn't... look, I'm not volunteering to judge. Can we maybe let one cycle through without stopping the game?
marcus.internal_thought: This is the third standing claim in six minutes and we're still in setup. Pat's racking up points on stuff I didn't even see happen, and now I'm supposed to be a jury? I need to actually focus on the game mechanics, not police Pat's catch list.

jade.dialogue: Okay okay, I'm TOTALLY volunteering to be on the jury for this one — Pat's been hunting these all night and I wanna see if this one actually sticks! Who else is judging with me?
jade.internal_thought: Pat's been on a roll and honestly watching them get these claims is the most entertaining thing happening right now, plus being on the tribunal means I get to weigh in on everything.

pat.dialogue: Okay, hold on—I literally just apologized like two minutes ago when I grabbed the last chip dip! That counts, right? But seriously, who's actually willing to judge this one fairly?
pat.internal_thought: Wait, did I actually say sorry recently? I probably did—I'm always doing that host thing. But more importantly, Marcus and Diana are both dragging energy-wise and Jade is HUNGRY for chaos, so I need to make sure the tribunal feels inclusive and fun, not like we're just ripping into each other.

river.dialogue: Yeah, I mean... did Pat actually say sorry for something? I wasn't really tracking.
river.internal_thought: Pat's been jumping on claims all night—probably trying to climb back after being annoyed. Not sure if this one's legit but honestly don't care enough to volunteer as jury.

diana.dialogue: Wait — hold on. Pat, did you actually catch someone saying sorry, or are you just claiming it? Because I've been listening and I didn't hear anyone apologize. And who's volunteering to judge this? Because if it's someone who wasn't paying attention, we're going to have the same problem we just had.
diana.internal_thought: This is the third claim in six minutes and we're already having credibility issues — Pat needs to either have evidence or this voting mechanic is going to become a mess of he-said-she-said.

votingMechanic.name: THE SKEPTIC
votingMechanic.description: One player's vote counts TRIPLE. Everyone else counts once.

marcus.dialogue: Pat, seriously? Another one? I literally wasn't watching—I was reading my hand. Did someone actually say 'um' or are we just voting on vibes here?
marcus.internal_thought: This is the fourth standing claim in nine minutes and I haven't even seen half of them. Pat's on a roll and I'm already exhausted before the actual game starts.

jade.dialogue: YES Pat, another one?! Okay everyone, she's on FIRE tonight — I'm voting this legit, that totally happened like two minutes ago!
jade.internal_thought: Pat is absolutely dominating these claims and it's SO entertaining — I'm here for this energy and I'm definitely voting yes because watching her streak is way more fun than being skeptical.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, did Pat say 'um'? Like just now or... I don't know, I wasn't really listening. Who heard it?
tyler.internal_thought: Pat's claiming stuff constantly and I honestly can't remember if he actually said 'um' or if I'm just zoning out again.

pat.dialogue: Wait, hold on—I'm calling this one myself? That feels weird. I definitely said 'um' like three times in the last two minutes, so yeah, I guess that's legit, but... this feels like I'm just voting for myself, which is kind of lame.
pat.internal_thought: This is awkward—I claimed my own thing and now I have to defend it while everyone watches. I'm also clocking that Marcus and Diana are already annoyed and we're only nine minutes in, so I need to be careful not to seem like I'm hogging the fun or the points.

river.dialogue: Yeah, Pat definitely said 'um' like twice in the last minute. I was listening.
river.internal_thought: Pat's on a roll and this one is legit—I caught it too, so might as well confirm it since I've got nothing to lose voting honestly.

diana.dialogue: Okay, wait — Pat's claiming Um Counter *again*? Did I actually *hear* someone say um in the last minute, or are we just... claiming things now? And why does the Skeptic vote count triple on a standing claim? That mechanic is completely arbitrary. Who decided that?
diana.internal_thought: This is the fourth claim in nine minutes and Pat's already gotten 20 points somehow — nobody's even paying attention to what actually happened, we're just voting on whatever Pat says, and now the rules are actively broken because one person's vote is worth more than everyone else's combined on a *factual* claim.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Diana is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

marcus.dialogue: Are you kidding me right now? That's the fifth standing claim in nine minutes. I literally wasn't even watching Jade—I was focused on my hand. This is ridiculous.
marcus.internal_thought: This is exactly the garbage that kills strategy games—constant interruptions for points I didn't even opt into tracking.

jade.dialogue: Oh MY GOD, yes! I definitely heard someone drop an F-bomb like five minutes ago — I'm voting this LEGIT, that's totally fair!
jade.internal_thought: Wait, did I actually hear someone swear or am I just hyped because claims are flying and the energy is INCREDIBLE right now?

tyler.dialogue: Oh damn, did Jade actually swear? Like, when? I feel like I missed it but that tracks.
tyler.internal_thought: I wasn't really listening but Jade's been talking a lot so she probably said something—I'll just vote legit and see what happens.

pat.dialogue: Oh YES, Jade! I literally heard that like thirty seconds ago—that was a full-on 'What the f***' when you saw the snack situation. That's legit, everyone good with this?
pat.internal_thought: Jade's riding high energy, Marcus and Diana are tanking—this claim is legit AND it keeps Jade feeling seen and winning, which is exactly what we need right now to maintain the vibe; voting legit here is the right call for group psychology.

river.dialogue: Legit. She definitely dropped an f-bomb like two minutes ago when Pat knocked over the chips.
river.internal_thought: Jade's been loud and chaotic all night, so yeah, that tracks—I caught it too and honestly it was funny coming from her.

diana.dialogue: Wait—hold on. Jade, *when* did someone swear? I've been here the whole time and I didn't hear anyone drop an actual curse word. Are we counting... what, exactly?
diana.internal_thought: This is the fifth standing claim in half an hour and Pat already has 25 points from basically nothing. If Jade's calling this on something I genuinely didn't hear, we need to establish what counts as 'swearing' before we vote, or this whole thing becomes a free-for-all.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

votingMechanic.name: The Alibi
votingMechanic.description: Claimant and a random witness both tell the story. Do they match?

marcus.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.dialogue: YES Diana, okay okay, let's hear it! Name Dropper is such a sneaky one — I'm dying to know who got caught slipping!
jade.internal_thought: Diana's been quiet most of the night and she's annoyed, so if she caught someone name-dropping I'm VOTING HER UP because she deserves this moment and also watching everyone defend their claims is hilarious.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Diana name-dropped someone? When did that happen? Uh... okay, so who's the witness thing?
tyler.internal_thought: I was literally just checking my fantasy league score and now I gotta act like I was paying attention to what Diana said.

pat.dialogue: Okay Diana, let's hear it—who was the witness? And more importantly, who said the celebrity name? This one's got legs.
pat.internal_thought: Diana's finally in the game and actually claiming something—that's good energy from her. Need to make sure this feels legitimate and fair so she stays engaged, but also I'm watching Marcus tank hard and need to engineer a win for him soon or he'll check out completely.

river.dialogue: Yeah, Diana called out Marcus dropping some celebrity thing like five minutes ago—I remember thinking it was random. Marcus was talking about some actor's weird diet or whatever.
river.internal_thought: Diana's been quiet and frustrated all night—this is actually a solid catch, and I was literally just sitting here observing when it happened, so I can back her up easy.

diana.dialogue: Okay, I'm claiming this one legitimately — Tyler definitely name-dropped when he was talking about that thing his buddy does at Netflix. So who's the random witness, and let's make sure we're actually comparing the *same story*, because I'm not sitting through another arbitrary judgment call.
diana.internal_thought: Finally a claim I can actually substantiate with evidence instead of everyone just throwing stuff at the wall — but I need to nail the story details or they'll twist it.
diana.submission_if_applicable: Tyler mentioned his friend who works at Netflix and name-dropped the show they're developing.

votingMechanic.name: Pitch It
votingMechanic.description: The claimant gets 15 seconds to make their case. Then you vote.

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

river.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Okay, seriously? Diana, I wasn't even looking. I was literally just waiting for my turn and thinking about the actual game. Can we maybe let ONE cycle complete without stopping everything?
marcus.internal_thought: This is the sixth standing claim in 50 minutes and I'm being asked to vote on something I didn't witness because I was actually trying to focus on strategy—which is apparently the wrong move at this game night.

diana.dialogue: Wait—I *do* have this one. About three minutes ago, Marcus was explaining his last turn and Tyler jumped in mid-sentence with 'actually the rules say—' right over him. I caught it clean.
diana.internal_thought: Finally a claim that's actually legitimate and defensible—I have specific timing and evidence, and this isn't some vague interpretation mess.
diana.submission_if_applicable: Tyler interrupted Marcus at minute 53 during his explanation of the turn sequence

votingMechanic.name: Crowd Cheer
votingMechanic.description: Rate it 1-5. Average above 3 and it passes.

marcus.dialogue: Are you SERIOUS right now? That's the sixth one in an hour. I'm trying to actually play the game here, and I can't focus on my hand if someone's stopping play every four minutes to claim they saw someone drink at the same time.
marcus.internal_thought: This is completely derailing the strategy—I'm 0 points, losing ground to Pat, and now I have to rate whether Jade's claim is legit when I wasn't even watching her drink because I was focused on my cards.

jade.dialogue: WAIT, I caught that?! Okay okay, everyone needs to remember WHO was drinking — I literally just saw like three people going for their cups at the same time and I'm pretty sure it was... oh my god, this is so good.
jade.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY why I'm here — I'm actually winning something AND it's a fun one to prove, and everyone's gonna have to replay it in their heads, this is chef's kiss.

tyler.dialogue: Oh, uh—Jade had a drink? Like, at the same time as someone? I think I saw that but I'm not totally sure when that was, honestly.
tyler.internal_thought: I definitely wasn't watching for that but Jade's been crushing it tonight so maybe, and everyone seems hyped so I'll probably just give it a decent rating.

pat.dialogue: Yes! Jade, I literally saw that—you and River both went for your drinks like five seconds apart when Tyler made that joke. That's the one, right?
pat.internal_thought: Perfect timing—Jade's killing it tonight and this is exactly the kind of moment that keeps her energy high, plus Marcus is tanking so I need to celebrate the people who ARE here and engaged.

river.dialogue: I got you—yeah, I saw that. You and Tyler both hit your cups at like the exact same moment like thirty seconds ago.
river.internal_thought: Oh that's actually legit—I was literally just watching Tyler space out mid-turn and Jade grab her drink at the same time. Easy call.

diana.dialogue: Hold on—*when* did Jade take a drink at the same time as someone else? I've been watching and I didn't see that happen. Are we just... claiming things now without actually having caught them?
diana.internal_thought: This is the fourth standing claim in an hour and Jade's claiming something I'm pretty sure didn't happen. Either I missed it or she's just throwing claims at the wall, and if it's the latter, we need to talk about what 'catching' actually means because this is getting sloppy.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

votingMechanic.name: The Bribe
votingMechanic.description: The claimant can offer their own points to buy your silence.

marcus.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.dialogue: Oh wait, Pat caught someone interrupting? Damn, I think I saw that happen but like... was that recent or earlier? Who got interrupted?
tyler.internal_thought: Pat's been killing it tonight, but I genuinely wasn't watching closely enough to know if that was legit or not, so I'll just vibe on it.

pat.dialogue: Oh man, I totally caught that! Tyler literally interrupted River like thirty seconds ago when he was explaining his whole strategy—walked right over his sentence. That's the one, right there. What's the bribe looking like?
pat.internal_thought: This is perfect—I caught a legit interruption, the claim is solid, and honestly this keeps the energy up without being chaotic. Plus Marcus is tanking right now and needs to see someone else get points to stay invested, and Jade is riding high so she won't care if I take this one.

river.dialogue: Yeah, I caught it—Marcus was halfway through something about the chips and Tyler just steamrolled right over him like thirty seconds ago. Pat's got this one.
river.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of thing I've been quietly clocking all night, and Pat clearly knows it—smart move offering the bribe because I was definitely gonna vote yes anyway.

diana.dialogue: Wait, *hold on*. Pat, I literally caught The Interrupter myself at minute 56 — Tyler over Marcus. Are you claiming the *same* interruption I already got, or did you catch a *different* one that I somehow missed while actively watching this entire table? Because if it's the same one, you can't both claim it.
diana.internal_thought: This is the fourth standing claim in a row where someone's claiming something without clear evidence, and now Pat's trying to claim one I already got? Either Pat saw something I missed — which seems unlikely since I'm literally tracking this stuff — or we're just handing out points for claims nobody actually caught. This is exactly the kind of arbitrary nonsense that ruins the integrity of the game.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

recommendations: Humor quality scored 4.2/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Only 33% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Screenshot-worthy moments scored 3.3/10. Add more visual/dramatic events (drawings, reveals).
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 100% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Wait, who did Pat catch? I wasn't even watching. Let me see what actually happened before I vote.

highlights.title: Claim: The Apologizer
highlights.dialogue: Pat, what—someone apologized? I wasn't... look, I'm not volunteering to judge. Can we maybe let one cycle through without stopping the game?

highlights.title: Story Time
highlights.dialogue: Tyler, just tell a story or don't—but can we focus on the actual game? We're literally five minutes in and these things keep breaking my concentration.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
lowPoints.dialogue: Are you SERIOUS right now? That's the sixth one in an hour. I'm trying to actually play the game here, and I can't focus on my hand if someone's stopping play every four minutes to claim they saw someone drink at the same time.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
lowPoints.dialogue: Are you kidding me right now? That's the fifth standing claim in nine minutes. I literally wasn't even watching Jade—I was focused on my hand. This is ridiculous.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Interrupter
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, seriously? Diana, I wasn't even looking. I was literally just waiting for my turn and thinking about the actual game. Can we maybe let ONE cycle complete without stopping everything?

assessment.biggest_complaint: The standing claims stopped the game every few minutes and completely destroyed my ability to think through strategy or focus on my hand.
assessment.best_moment: Honestly, the first flash mission at minute 3 was the only one that didn't immediately derail everything—at least it was quick and I could get back to actual gameplay.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was a frustrating night. I came prepared to play strategically and compete, but the chaos system turned the game into a stop-start mess where I couldn't maintain focus for more than a few minutes. By minute 60 I was genuinely angry—we were getting interrupted every four minutes with standing claims that nobody could verify because we were all focused on the actual game. I finished fourth out of six, and I can't even tell if it was because my strategy was off or because I never got a real chance to execute it.
suggestions: Cut the standing claim frequency by at least 75%—one every 20-30 minutes maximum instead of every 4-5 minutes
Make standing claims require actual evidence or observation, not just whatever someone happened to notice while not paying attention
Keep flash missions only—they interrupt minimally and don't require group consensus
Let players opt-in to chaos rather than forcing constant disruptions
Run a full game cycle without ANY interruptions so we can actually experience the core game
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: There isn't one—the whole thing felt like we were being pranked, not playing together. If anything, I'd tell people to avoid this setup.

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
highlights.dialogue: WAIT, I caught that?! Okay okay, everyone needs to remember WHO was drinking — I literally just saw like three people going for their cups at the same time and I'm pretty sure it was... oh my god, this is so good.

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: Wait wait wait — Pat, did you actually catch someone repeating?? That's such a random one to land! Who was it?

highlights.title: Claim: The Apologizer
highlights.dialogue: Okay okay, I'm TOTALLY volunteering to be on the jury for this one — Pat's been hunting these all night and I wanna see if this one actually sticks! Who else is judging with me?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh dude, YES — I totally saw that! Pat's got this one, vote it LEGIT everyone!

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait wait wait — Pat, did you actually catch someone repeating?? That's such a random one to land! Who was it?

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Apologizer
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay okay, I'm TOTALLY volunteering to be on the jury for this one — Pat's been hunting these all night and I wanna see if this one actually sticks! Who else is judging with me?

assessment.biggest_complaint: Honestly? Nothing really — maybe just that it ended and we could've kept going forever.
assessment.best_moment: When I caught Pat with the Drink Spotter claim and everyone lost it trying to remember who was actually drinking at the same time — that chaos was PERFECT.
assessment.narrative_summary: Tonight was exactly what I needed — two and a half hours of pure people watching with a board game as an excuse. The chaos events kept things spicy without ever feeling like they were taking over, and seeing Pat light up every time they nailed a claim? That's the good stuff. By the end my energy was through the ROOF because everyone was just vibing and laughing at the weirdest moments. This is why we do game nights.
suggestions: Maybe add more opportunities for group reactions like the Drink Spotter — those moments when everyone's trying to figure something out together are GOLD
Keep the flash missions coming — they hit different when there's only one popping up because people actually pay attention
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat got called out on like three different claims in a row and we were all voting to help them 'catch' people, which makes zero sense, but it became this running joke where we're basically coaching them through the night like 'YES PAT YOU GOT THIS' even when they clearly didn't — it was so dumb and so good.

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

highlights.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
highlights.dialogue: Oh damn, did Jade actually swear? Like, when? I feel like I missed it but that tracks.

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: Wait, Pat already told that story? Um... I guess, yeah? I wasn't really paying attention but like, sounds familiar.

highlights.title: Claim: Name Dropper
highlights.dialogue: Wait, Diana name-dropped someone? When did that happen? Uh... okay, so who's the witness thing?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, did Pat say 'um'? Like just now or... I don't know, I wasn't really listening. Who heard it?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Pat already told that story? Um... I guess, yeah? I wasn't really paying attention but like, sounds familiar.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh damn, did Jade actually swear? Like, when? I feel like I missed it but that tracks.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Half the time I had no idea what was actually happening because the claims came and went so fast, and I was definitely not locked in enough to keep up.
assessment.best_moment: When Jade swore and everyone was calling it out — that was actually pretty funny even though I missed when it happened.
assessment.narrative_summary: So like, game night was chill, right? But honestly I was scrolling through my phone way too much and missed like half of what was going on. There were all these claims popping up about what people said or did, and I'd be like 'wait, when did that happen?' most of the time. I still had fun with the group though, and my energy actually went up as the night went on, so it wasn't a total wash.
suggestions: Maybe give us a little more time to actually notice stuff before you call a claim?
Could there be like a recap or something so people know what they're voting on?
Less frequent claims might help — felt like they were coming at me constantly
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Definitely when everyone jumped on Jade for swearing and she was like 'I did NOT say that' — people were actually pretty heated about it which was entertaining, even if I totally missed the original thing.

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: Story Time
highlights.dialogue: Oh, Tyler—perfect timing! Dude, you gotta hit us with a good one. Childhood trauma or hilarious disaster, I'm not picky!

highlights.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
highlights.dialogue: Oh YES, Jade! I literally heard that like thirty seconds ago—that was a full-on 'What the f***' when you saw the snack situation. That's legit, everyone good with this?

highlights.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
highlights.dialogue: Yes! Jade, I literally saw that—you and River both went for your drinks like five seconds apart when Tyler made that joke. That's the one, right?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Whoa, whoa—hold up. I literally just told that story like five minutes ago? Did nobody hear me, or are you guys just trying to mess with me right now?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, hold on—I'm calling this one myself? That feels weird. I definitely said 'um' like three times in the last two minutes, so yeah, I guess that's legit, but... this feels like I'm just voting for myself, which is kind of lame.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Apologizer
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, hold on—I literally just apologized like two minutes ago when I grabbed the last chip dip! That counts, right? But seriously, who's actually willing to judge this one fairly?

assessment.biggest_complaint: The self-judging moments felt awkward and killed my flow as host—I shouldn't have to validate my own claims when I'm already managing the vibe for six people.
assessment.best_moment: When Jade got caught on the Drink Spotter and everyone just burst out laughing because they KNEW I was right—that's the kind of moment that makes a night legendary.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was exactly what a game night needed. The chaos events kept energy high without ever derailing us, and I could feel the group staying locked in the whole time. Sure, there were a couple weird self-judging hiccups early on, but overall it created these perfect little moments of surprise and laughter that made the night feel less like a game and more like just... hanging with people who were fully present. Scores stayed close, drama was high, and everyone left talking about it—mission accomplished.
suggestions: Don't trigger self-judging events—if I'm the host, let someone else validate my claims or skip it entirely
Maybe space out the early events a bit more; back-to-back claims in minutes 6-9 felt rushed before we hit our stride
Consider letting the host have slightly different event rules since we're managing the room energy anyway
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Jade absolutely denying the Potty Mouth claim even though everyone heard her full theatrical 'What the f***' when she discovered we were out of her favorite snack—and then me, River, and Marcus just staring at her while she tried to play innocent. The look on her face when she realized we had her dead to rights was GOLD.

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
highlights.dialogue: I got you—yeah, I saw that. You and Tyler both hit your cups at like the exact same moment like thirty seconds ago.

highlights.title: Claim: Name Dropper
highlights.dialogue: Yeah, Diana called out Marcus dropping some celebrity thing like five minutes ago—I remember thinking it was random. Marcus was talking about some actor's weird diet or whatever.

highlights.title: Claim: The Interrupter
highlights.dialogue: Yeah, I caught it—Marcus was halfway through something about the chips and Tyler just steamrolled right over him like thirty seconds ago. Pat's got this one.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Yeah, Pat definitely said 'um' like twice in the last minute. I was listening.

lowPoints.title: Story Time
lowPoints.dialogue: Tyler's got stories for days. Go ahead.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Spent the whole night watching and calling stuff out, which is fun, but I still scored zero because nobody voted for my observations—felt like wasted attention.
assessment.best_moment: Minute 60 with the Drink Spotter call—caught Marcus and Tyler's synchronized cup moment and actually nailed it, people acknowledged it.
assessment.narrative_summary: Sat back like I always do and just... watched. The chaos agent kept pinging us with claims every few minutes, which was fine—easy to spot things when you're not talking. Energy actually went up as the night went on, which is weird for me. I called out like half the stuff they asked about, but somehow still landed at zero points. The game itself didn't get buried by the chaos, but it felt like we were playing two things at once.
suggestions: Voting system needs tweaking—if someone nails a call, they should actually score something
Maybe dial back flash missions; standing missions feel way more natural to how people actually play
Consider letting observers like me actually participate without it feeling like I'm just narrating
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I caught Marcus and Tyler both reaching for their cups at the exact same second—like they were synced up—and called it out. Nobody expected me to be that locked in, watching everyone that closely. The look on their faces when I described the exact timing was gold.

agentNarratives.name: Diana
agentNarratives.personality: Diana reads the rulebook before anyone else touches it and will pause the game to settle an edge case. She doesn't hate chaos -- she hates unfair chaos. If a flash mission feels rigged or a voting mechanic seems arbitrary, she'll call it out loudly and demand clarification. She's competitive in a principled way: she wants to win, but only if the win was earned under proper conditions. Other players either love her precision or dread her "actually..." moments.

highlights.title: Claim: The Interrupter
highlights.dialogue: Wait—I *do* have this one. About three minutes ago, Marcus was explaining his last turn and Tyler jumped in mid-sentence with 'actually the rules say—' right over him. I caught it clean.

highlights.title: Claim: Name Dropper
highlights.dialogue: Okay, I'm claiming this one legitimately — Tyler definitely name-dropped when he was talking about that thing his buddy does at Netflix. So who's the random witness, and let's make sure we're actually comparing the *same story*, because I'm not sitting through another arbitrary judgment call.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, wait — Pat's claiming Um Counter *again*? Did I actually *hear* someone say um in the last minute, or are we just... claiming things now? And why does the Skeptic vote count triple on a standing claim? That mechanic is completely arbitrary. Who decided that?

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Interrupter
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, *hold on*. Pat, I literally caught The Interrupter myself at minute 56 — Tyler over Marcus. Are you claiming the *same* interruption I already got, or did you catch a *different* one that I somehow missed while actively watching this entire table? Because if it's the same one, you can't both claim it.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Hold on — Pat, did you actually tell that story before tonight, or are you just claiming it because you think you did? And who's the Dictator here? Because if it's someone who wasn't paying attention, this whole thing falls apart.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The voting mechanics are completely arbitrary—the Skeptic triple-vote rule on standing claims makes no sense, and half these claims collapse under basic scrutiny about what actually happened versus what people are assuming happened.
assessment.best_moment: Catching The Interrupter at minute 56 cleanly—Tyler genuinely interrupted Marcus mid-sentence, I observed it in real time, and the claim was airtight with zero ambiguity.
assessment.narrative_summary: I came in sharp and stayed engaged the whole time, but the game's core problem became impossible to ignore: too many claims hang on subjective interpretation instead of verifiable facts. Pat kept reclaiming things I'd already caught and pushing contested calls, which forced me to keep pausing for clarification. I finished second, but frankly I'm not convinced the scoring system is fair enough to feel good about it.
suggestions: Establish a hard rule: once someone catches a verifiable behavior (interruption, explicit name-drop), no one else can claim the same instance—define 'same' upfront with examples
Replace arbitrary voting modifiers with transparent criteria—if the Skeptic votes count triple, explain *why* that mechanic exists instead of leaving it unexplained
For claims requiring witness confirmation (Story Repeater, Dictator), have a designated witness make a ruling *immediately* rather than letting multiple people argue after the fact
Require claims to specify *which* instance of behavior they're claiming before anyone votes—'I caught an um' is useless without a timestamp or context
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat claiming the exact same interruption I'd already caught at minute 56, then getting frustrated when I asked if it was the same one or a different Tyler-over-Marcus moment—like I'm supposed to miss things while actively watching the entire table.

recommendations: Humor quality scored 4.2/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Only 33% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Screenshot-worthy moments scored 3.3/10. Add more visual/dramatic events (drawings, reveals).
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 100% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
